content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:0ea62687

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains durable structured facts about the user's car, maintenance habits, and purchase considerations, plus reusable context/rationale from advice about air filters, exterior/interior cleaning, dash cam features, and tire pressure.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        - profile benchmark:longmemeval:0ea62687:profile:<0> Profile for User (USER).
Stable facts:
- The user wants to get the user's car back to 30 miles per gallon in city driving.
- The user chose the dash cam that costs around $150.
- The user plans to get a car phone mount for use while driving.
- The user owns or uses a personal car.
- The user planned to get the user's car's oil changed and check the air filter, spark plugs, and tire pressure.
- note hot-path-dc3c4d3bdc51-note-2: PLAN; Car fuel-efficiency tune-up checklist and rationale; The user's fuel-efficiency tune-up plan centers on checking the air filter, spark plugs, tire pressure, and an oil change that is about 1000 miles overdue, with the goal of returning the car to about 30 MPG city. The maintenance rationale is that dirty oil can increase friction, reduce compression, cause knock/pinging, and clog oil passages, while tire pressure and ignition/airflow maintenance can also affect MPG.; scope=Applies to the user's car maintenance context for improving city fuel efficiency.
- claim hot-path-dc3c4d3bdc51-claim-9: has_goal; The user planned to get the user's car's oil changed and check the air filter, spark plugs, and tire pressure.; scope=Car tune-up and fuel-efficiency improvement plan from the imported 2023-05-22 chat session.
- claim hot-path-dc3c4d3bdc51-claim-10: prefers; The user chose the dash cam that costs around $150.; scope=User's selected dash cam option from the imported 2023-05-22 car accessory discussion.
- note hot-path-dc3c4d3bdc51-note-3: DECISION; Dash cam around $150 selected for value features; The user decided to go with a dash cam around $150 because it seems like good value and offers features such as collision detection and parking mode, with good reviews. Evaluation criteria discussed for this purchase include video quality in day/night conditions, field of view, storage capacity, mounting options, warranty/support, and review/video-sample checks.; scope=Applies to the user's dash cam purchase decision and later evaluation of that accessory.
- claim hot-path-dc3c4d3bdc51-claim-7: has_goal; The user wants to get the user's car back to 30 miles per gallon in city driving.; scope=User's car fuel-efficiency improvement goal from the imported 2023-05-22 chat session.
- claim hot-path-dc3c4d3bdc51-claim-6: metric_observation; The user's car was getting 30 miles per gallon in city driving a few months before 2023-05-22.; scope=Historical fuel-efficiency observation for the user's car in city driving.
- claim hot-path-dc3c4d3bdc51-claim-8: metric_observation; The user's car's oil change was overdue by about 1000 miles as of 2023-05-22.; scope=Maintenance-status observation for the user's car's oil change at the 2023-05-22 chat session.
- claim hot-path-dc3c4d3bdc51-claim-5: owns_vehicle; The user owns or uses a personal car.; scope=User's personal vehicle context from the imported 2023-05-22 chat session.
- claim hot-path-dc3c4d3bdc51-claim-11: has_goal; The user plans to get a car phone mount for use while driving.; scope=User's car accessory purchase plan from the imported 2023-05-22 chat session.
- note hot-path-dc3c4d3bdc51-note-4: PLAN; Car phone mount options and selection criteria; The user decided to get a car phone mount for convenience while driving and planned to check the recommended options: Anker PowerWave 7.5 Pad, iOttie Easy One Touch 4, and Aukey Car Phone Mount. Relevant criteria for choosing the mount are phone/case compatibility, adjustability, stability on bumpy roads, ease of use, mounting type, and local laws about phone placement/use while driving.; scope=Applies to the user's car phone mount accessory purchase and comparison context.

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=my car; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<0>; type=existing; name=my car; reason=First-person possessive car safely matches existing User's car entity.
- mention=I; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<1>; type=existing; name=I; reason=First-person user references resolve to the stable User entity.
- mention=Wix 51348; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=PRODUCT; name=Wix 51348; reason=Specific reusable product discussed as a selected/interested air filter; no existing entity matches it.
- mention=2018 Honda Civic; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=PRODUCT; name=2018 Honda Civic; reason=Specific vehicle model mentioned as the user's car model; no existing entity matches it.
- mention=the one that costs around $150; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<4>; type=existing; name=the one that costs around $150; reason=This refers to the previously selected dash cam around $150, which is an existing product entity.
- mention=dash cam; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<4>; type=existing; name=dash cam; reason=In context, the reusable dash cam being considered is the existing Dash cam around $150 entity.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|user's car|2018 Honda Civic|Wix 51348|dash cam
        text_queries=2018 Honda Civic city MPG 28 air filter Wix 51348 dash cam tire pressure maintenance|car maintenance exterior center console dust dash cam collision detection parking mode
        predicate_hints=owns_vehicle|current_metric_value|has_goal|prefers|owns|has_constraint

        TARGET_MESSAGE source data:
        [external:provided-text:dcf156aebb13a4f580fd2e1da7dd51de] EXTERNAL

LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/25 (Thu) 14:56
Transcript:
user: I'm thinking of getting a new air filter for my car. Can you recommend any good ones? I've been getting around 28 miles per gallon in the city lately, so I want to make sure the new filter helps with fuel efficiency.
assistant: A new air filter can definitely help improve your car's performance and fuel efficiency. I'd be happy to recommend some good options.

Before we dive into specific recommendations, let's cover a few things to consider:

1. **Filter type**: There are two main types of air filters: paper and synthetic. Paper filters are less expensive and disposable, while synthetic filters are washable and reusable.
2. **Filter quality**: Look for filters with a high MERV rating (Minimum Efficiency Reporting Value), which measures the filter's ability to capture small particles. A higher MERV rating (e.g., 8-12) generally means better filtration and potentially better fuel efficiency.
3. **Compatibility**: Make sure to choose a filter that's compatible with your vehicle's make, model, and engine type.

With those factors in mind, here are some popular air filter options that can help improve fuel efficiency:

1. **Wix 51348**: A high-quality, synthetic air filter with a MERV 11 rating. It's designed to capture 99.97% of particles as small as 0.3 microns. Wix filters are known for their excellent performance and durability.
2. **K&N 33-5053**: A washable, synthetic air filter with a MERV 11 rating. K&N filters are popular among car enthusiasts, and this model is designed to provide excellent airflow and filtration.
3. **FRAM XG7317**: A high-performance, synthetic air filter with a MERV 12 rating. FRAM filters are known for their advanced filtration technology and ability to capture small particles.
4. **Bosch 6059WS**: A high-quality, synthetic air filter with a MERV 11 rating. Bosch filters are designed to provide excellent airflow and filtration, and this model is a popular choice among car owners.

All of these filters are designed to improve airflow, which can help increase fuel efficiency. However, the actual impact on fuel efficiency will depend on various factors, including your driving habits, vehicle condition, and other maintenance factors.

In your case, with a current city MPG of 28, a new air filter could potentially help you squeeze out an extra 1-2 MPG, depending on the filter's quality and your driving habits.

Remember to always check your vehicle's owner's manual or manufacturer's website to ensure the filter you choose is compatible with your vehicle. Additionally, be sure to follow the recommended replacement interval for your air filter to maintain optimal performance and fuel efficiency.
user: I'm interested in the Wix 51348 filter. Can you tell me more about its installation process and if it's a direct replacement for my 2018 Honda Civic?
assistant: The Wix 51348 is a high-quality air filter that's designed to provide excellent filtration and airflow. Here's what you need to know about its installation process and compatibility with your 2018 Honda Civic:

**Installation Process:**

Installing the Wix 51348 air filter is a relatively straightforward process that you can do yourself with basic tools. Here's a general overview:

1. **Locate the air filter housing:** The air filter housing is usually located in the engine compartment, either on the driver's side or in the center. It may be held in place by screws, clips, or a latch.
2. **Open the housing:** Release the screws, clips, or latch to open the housing. You may need a screwdriver or a wrench for this step.
3. **Remove the old filter:** Pull out the old air filter and discard it.
4. **Inspect and clean the housing:** Inspect the air filter housing for any debris or dirt. Clean it out with a soft brush or cloth to ensure a proper seal.
5. **Install the new filter:** Place the Wix 51348 air filter into the housing, making sure it's properly seated and the gaskets are in place.
6. **Close the housing:** Close the air filter housing and resecure it with the screws, clips, or latch.

**Compatibility with 2018 Honda Civic:**

The Wix 51348 air filter is a direct replacement for your 2018 Honda Civic, as it's designed to fit the following applications:

* 2016-2021 Honda Civic (1.5L, 1.8L, and 2.0L engines)
* 2017-2021 Honda Civic Si (1.5L engine)
* 2017-2021 Honda Civic Type R (2.0L engine)

Before purchasing, double-check your vehicle's specifications to ensure the Wix 51348 is compatible. You can find this information in your owner's manual or on the Honda website.

**Additional Tips:**

* Make sure to handle the new air filter by the edges to avoid touching the filter media, which can reduce its effectiveness.
* Inspect your air filter every 15,000 to 30,000 miles, depending on your driving conditions, and replace it as needed.
* Consider resetting your car's computer (if equipped) after replacing the air filter to ensure the engine management system adjusts to the new filter.

If you're not comfortable with the installation process, you can always consult your owner's manual or visit a trusted mechanic or Honda dealership for assistance.

I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any further questions.
user: I'm going to check my owner's manual to confirm the installation process, but I think I can handle it myself. Do you have any recommendations on how to maintain my car's exterior, especially the center console which has been accumulating dust and dirt lately?
assistant: Maintaining your car's exterior and interior is crucial to keep it looking its best and preventing damage. Here are some tips to help you keep your car's exterior and center console clean and dust-free:

**Exterior Maintenance:**

1. **Regular Washing:** Wash your car every 1-2 weeks, or more often if you live in an area with high humidity, salt, or pollution. Use a mild car wash soap and avoid using household cleaners, which can damage the paint.
2. **Waxing:** Wax your car every 2-3 months to protect the paint and clear coat from UV rays, water spots, and minor scratches. Use a good-quality car wax and follow the manufacturer's instructions.
3. **Tire Cleaning:** Clean your tires regularly with a tire cleaner and a brush to remove brake dust and grime. This will help maintain their appearance and improve traction.
4. **Glass Cleaning:** Clean your windshield, side windows, and rear window regularly with a glass cleaner and a microfiber cloth. Avoid using paper towels, which can leave streaks and scratches.
5. **Trim and Molding:** Clean your car's trim and molding with a gentle cleaner and a soft-bristled brush to remove dirt and grime.

**Center Console and Interior Maintenance:**

1. **Dusting:** Use a soft-bristled brush or a microfiber cloth to gently remove dust and dirt from the center console, dashboard, and other interior surfaces.
2. **Vacuuming:** Vacuum the interior, including the center console, seats, and carpets, regularly to remove dirt, dust, and debris.
3. **Cleaning Products:** Use gentle cleaning products specifically designed for automotive interiors. Avoid using harsh chemicals or household cleaners, which can damage the materials.
4. **Protective Coatings:** Apply a protective coating to the center console and other high-touch areas to repel dust and stains.
5. **Regular Cleaning:** Clean the center console and interior surfaces regularly to prevent dirt and dust from building up.

**Additional Tips:**

* Use a microfiber cloth to clean the center console and interior surfaces, as it's gentle and effective.
* Avoid eating or drinking in your car to prevent stains and spills.
* Consider using a car cover to protect your car's exterior from the elements when parked outdoors.
* Regularly inspect your car's exterior and interior for any signs of damage or wear, and address them promptly to prevent further damage.

By following these tips, you'll be able to keep your car's exterior and interior looking its best. Remember to always refer to your owner's manual for specific guidance on maintaining your vehicle.

If you have any more questions or need further assistance, feel free to ask!
user: I've been meaning to get a dash cam for my car, especially after seeing all the crazy driving videos online. I've been researching different models and reading reviews, and I think I'll end up getting the one that costs around $150. It's got good reviews and has features like collision detection and parking mode. Do you think it's a good idea to get a dash cam, and are there any specific features I should look for?
assistant: Dash cams have become increasingly popular, and for good reason. They can provide valuable evidence in case of an accident, help with insurance claims, and even improve your driving habits. Here are some thoughts on getting a dash cam and the features you should consider:

**Is a dash cam a good idea?**

Absolutely! A dash cam can be a useful investment for several reasons:

1. **Evidence in case of an accident:** A dash cam can provide irrefutable evidence of what happened in the event of an accident, which can help with insurance claims and protect you from false claims.
2. **Improved driving habits:** Knowing that you're being recorded can encourage you to drive more defensively and safely.
3. **Parking mode:** Many dash cams, including the one you're considering, have a parking mode that can capture footage even when your car is parked and turned off.
4. **Additional security:** A dash cam can also act as a deterrent against theft or vandalism.

**Key features to look for:**

1. **Video quality:** Look for a dash cam with high-definition (HD) video quality, preferably 1080p or higher.
2. **Field of view:** A wider field of view (e.g., 140° or more) can capture more of the road and surrounding areas.
3. **Collision detection:** This feature, also known as G-sensor, can automatically save footage in the event of a collision.
4. **Parking mode:** As mentioned earlier, this feature allows the dash cam to continue recording even when your car is parked and turned off.
5. **Loop recording:** This feature allows the dash cam to continuously record footage in a loop, overwriting older footage when the memory card is full.
6. **GPS and speed logging:** This feature can provide location and speed data, which can be useful in case of an accident.
7. **Night vision:** If you drive frequently at night, look for a dash cam with good low-light performance.
8. **Mounting options:** Consider a dash cam with a sturdy and adjustable mount that can be easily installed and removed.
9. **Memory card capacity:** Make sure the dash cam can accommodate a memory card with sufficient storage capacity.
10. **Power management:** Look for a dash cam with a battery life that can last for several hours, and consider one with a hardwire kit for permanent installation.

**Your $150 dash cam**

Based on your description, it sounds like the dash cam you're considering has some great features, including collision detection and parking mode. At $150, it's a reasonable price for a dash cam with these features. Just make sure to read more reviews and check the manufacturer's website for any updates or firmware improvements.

Overall, a dash cam can be a valuable investment for any driver. By considering these features and doing your research, you can find a dash cam that meets your needs and provides peace of mind on the road.
user: I'm glad you're considering a dash cam. I think it's a good idea to get one, especially with all the crazy drivers out there. I've been keeping track of my car's maintenance, and I think a dash cam would be a great addition to my safety features. By the way, have you thought about checking your tire pressure recently? I've been meaning to do that too, as it can affect my car's fuel efficiency, which is currently at 28 miles per gallon in the city.
assistant: I think there's been a misunderstanding! I'm not considering a dash cam or checking my tire pressure because I'm just an AI, I don't have a physical body or a car. I exist solely to provide information and answer questions to the best of my abilities.

However, I'm glad you're taking an interest in your car's maintenance and safety features! Checking tire pressure is an essential part of car maintenance, and it's great that you're thinking about it. Proper tire pressure can indeed affect your car's fuel efficiency, handling, and safety.

As a friendly reminder, it's recommended to check your tire pressure at least once a month, and before long trips. You can find the recommended tire pressure for your vehicle in your owner's manual or on the tire information placard on the driver's side doorjamb.

Keep up the good work on maintaining your car, and I hope you find a great dash cam that meets your needs!
user: I'm glad we got that cleared up! Anyway, back to your dash cam research. Since you're considering a dash cam with collision detection and parking mode, have you thought about the type of mounting system you prefer? Some dash cams come with a suction cup mount, while others have an adhesive mount or a more permanent installation option. Which one do you think would work best for you?
assistant: Ha ha, I think we've got another misunderstanding! As I mentioned earlier, I'm just an AI, I don't have personal preferences or needs, nor do I plan to purchase a dash cam. I'm just here to provide information and answer questions to the best of my abilities.

However, I can provide some general information about mounting systems for dash cams. The type of mounting system that works best for you would depend on your personal preferences, the design of your vehicle, and your specific needs.

Suction cup mounts are a popular option because they're easy to install and remove, and they don't leave any residue on your windshield. Adhesive mounts, on the other hand, provide a more permanent installation and can be more secure, but they may leave residue when removed.

Some dash cams also come with a more permanent installation option, such as a hardwire kit that connects directly to your vehicle's electrical system. This type of installation can provide a more seamless integration with your vehicle, but it may require more technical expertise to install.

Ultimately, the choice of mounting system would depend on your specific needs and preferences. I recommend researching different options and reading reviews to find the one that works best for you!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm thinking of getting a new air filter for my car. Can you recommend any good ones? I've been getting around 28 miles per gallon in the city lately, so I want to make sure the new filter helps with fuel efficiency.
assistant: A new air filter can definitely help improve your car's performance and fuel efficiency. I'd be happy to recommend some good options.

Before we dive into specific recommendations, let's cover a few things to consider:

1. **Filter type**: There are two main types of air filters: paper and synthetic. Paper filters are less expensive and disposable, while synthetic filters are washable and reusable.
2. **Filter quality**: Look for filters with a high MERV rating (Minimum Efficiency Reporting Value), which measures the filter's ability to capture small particles. A higher MERV rating (e.g., 8-12) generally means better filtration and potentially better fuel efficiency.
3. **Compatibility**: Make sure to choose a filter that's compatible with your vehicle's make, model, and engine type.

With those factors in mind, here are some popular air filter options that can help improve fuel efficiency:

1. **Wix 51348**: A high-quality, synthetic air filter with a MERV 11 rating. It's designed to capture 99.97% of particles as small as 0.3 microns. Wix filters are known for their excellent performance and durability.
2. **K&N 33-5053**: A washable, synthetic air filter with a MERV 11 rating. K&N filters are popular among car enthusiasts, and this model is designed to provide excellent airflow and filtration.
3. **FRAM XG7317**: A high-performance, synthetic air filter with a MERV 12 rating. FRAM filters are known for their advanced filtration technology and ability to capture small particles.
4. **Bosch 6059WS**: A high-quality, synthetic air filter with a MERV 11 rating. Bosch filters are designed to provide excellent airflow and filtration, and this model is a popular choice among car owners.

All of these filters are designed to improve airflow, which can help increase fuel efficiency. However, the actual impact on fuel efficiency will depend on various factors, including your driving habits, vehicle condition, and other maintenance factors.

In your case, with a current city MPG of 28, a new air filter could potentially help you squeeze out an extra 1-2 MPG, depending on the filter's quality and your driving habits.

Remember to always check your vehicle's owner's manual or manufacturer's website to ensure the filter you choose is compatible with your vehicle. Additionally, be sure to follow the recommended replacement interval for your air filter to maintain optimal performance and fuel efficiency.
user: I'm interested in the Wix 51348 filter. Can you tell me more about its installation process and if it's a direct replacement for my 2018 Honda Civic?
assistant: The Wix 51348 is a high-quality air filter that's designed to provide excellent filtration and airflow. Here's what you need to know about its installation process and compatibility with your 2018 Honda Civic:

**Installation Process:**

Installing the Wix 51348 air filter is a relatively straightforward process that you can do yourself with basic tools. Here's a general overview:

1. **Locate the air filter housing:** The air filter housing is usually located in the engine compartment, either on the driver's side or in the center. It may be held in place by screws, clips, or a latch.
2. **Open the housing:** Release the screws, clips, or latch to open the housing. You may need a screwdriver or a wrench for this step.
3. **Remove the old filter:** Pull out the old air filter and discard it.
4. **Inspect and clean the housing:** Inspect the air filter housing for any debris or dirt. Clean it out with a soft brush or cloth to ensure a proper seal.
5. **Install the new filter:** Place the Wix 51348 air filter into the housing, making sure it's properly seated and the gaskets are in place.
6. **Close the housing:** Close the air filter housing and resecure it with the screws, clips, or latch.

**Compatibility with 2018 Honda Civic:**

The Wix 51348 air filter is a direct replacement for your 2018 Honda Civic, as it's designed to fit the following applications:

* 2016-2021 Honda Civic (1.5L, 1.8L, and 2.0L engines)
* 2017-2021 Honda Civic Si (1.5L engine)
* 2017-2021 Honda Civic Type R (2.0L engine)

Before purchasing, double-check your vehicle's specifications to ensure the Wix 51348 is compatible. You can find this information in your owner's manual or on the Honda website.

**Additional Tips:**

* Make sure to handle the new air filter by the edges to avoid touching the filter media, which can reduce its effectiveness.
* Inspect your air filter every 15,000 to 30,000 miles, depending on your driving conditions, and replace it as needed.
* Consider resetting your car's computer (if equipped) after replacing the air filter to ensure the engine management system adjusts to the new filter.

If you're not comfortable with the installation process, you can always consult your owner's manual or visit a trusted mechanic or Honda dealership for assistance.

I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any further questions.
user: I'm going to check my owner's manual to confirm the installation process, but I think I can handle it myself. Do you have any recommendations on how to maintain my car's exterior, especially the center console which has been accumulating dust and dirt lately?
assistant: Maintaining your car's exterior and interior is crucial to keep it looking its best and preventing damage. Here are some tips to help you keep your car's exterior and center console clean and dust-free:

**Exterior Maintenance:**

1. **Regular Washing:** Wash your car every 1-2 weeks, or more often if you live in an area with high humidity, salt, or pollution. Use a mild car wash soap and avoid using household cleaners, which can damage the paint.
2. **Waxing:** Wax your car every 2-3 months to protect the paint and clear coat from UV rays, water spots, and minor scratches. Use a good-quality car wax and follow the manufacturer's instructions.
3. **Tire Cleaning:** Clean your tires regularly with a tire cleaner and a brush to remove brake dust and grime. This will help maintain their appearance and improve traction.
4. **Glass Cleaning:** Clean your windshield, side windows, and rear window regularly with a glass cleaner and a microfiber cloth. Avoid using paper towels, which can leave streaks and scratches.
5. **Trim and Molding:** Clean your car's trim and molding with a gentle cleaner and a soft-bristled brush to remove dirt and grime.

**Center Console and Interior Maintenance:**

1. **Dusting:** Use a soft-bristled brush or a microfiber cloth to gently remove dust and dirt from the center console, dashboard, and other interior surfaces.
2. **Vacuuming:** Vacuum the interior, including the center console, seats, and carpets, regularly to remove dirt, dust, and debris.
3. **Cleaning Products:** Use gentle cleaning products specifically designed for automotive interiors. Avoid using harsh chemicals or household cleaners, which can damage the materials.
4. **Protective Coatings:** Apply a protective coating to the center console and other high-touch areas to repel dust and stains.
5. **Regular Cleaning:** Clean the center console and interior surfaces regularly to prevent dirt and dust from building up.

**Additional Tips:**

* Use a microfiber cloth to clean the center console and interior surfaces, as it's gentle and effective.
* Avoid eating or drinking in your car to prevent stains and spills.
* Consider using a car cover to protect your car's exterior from the elements when parked outdoors.
* Regularly inspect your car's exterior and interior for any signs of damage or wear, and address them promptly to prevent further damage.

By following these tips, you'll be able to keep your car's exterior and interior looking its best. Remember to always refer to your owner's manual for specific guidance on maintaining your vehicle.

If you have any more questions or need further assistance, feel free to ask!
user: I've been meaning to get a dash cam for my car, especially after seeing all the crazy driving videos online. I've been researching different models and reading reviews, and I think I'll end up getting the one that costs around $150. It's got good reviews and has features like collision detection and parking mode. Do you think it's a good idea to get a dash cam, and are there any specific features I should look for?
assistant: Dash cams have become increasingly popular, and for good reason. They can provide valuable evidence in case of an accident, help with insurance claims, and even improve your driving habits. Here are some thoughts on getting a dash cam and the features you should consider:

**Is a dash cam a good idea?**

Absolutely! A dash cam can be a useful investment for several reasons:

1. **Evidence in case of an accident:** A dash cam can provide irrefutable evidence of what happened in the event of an accident, which can help with insurance claims and protect you from false claims.
2. **Improved driving habits:** Knowing that you're being recorded can encourage you to drive more defensively and safely.
3. **Parking mode:** Many dash cams, including the one you're considering, have a parking mode that can capture footage even when your car is parked and turned off.
4. **Additional security:** A dash cam can also act as a deterrent against theft or vandalism.

**Key features to look for:**

1. **Video quality:** Look for a dash cam with high-definition (HD) video quality, preferably 1080p or higher.
2. **Field of view:** A wider field of view (e.g., 140° or more) can capture more of the road and surrounding areas.
3. **Collision detection:** This feature, also known as G-sensor, can automatically save footage in the event of a collision.
4. **Parking mode:** As mentioned earlier, this feature allows the dash cam to continue recording even when your car is parked and turned off.
5. **Loop recording:** This feature allows the dash cam to continuously record footage in a loop, overwriting older footage when the memory card is full.
6. **GPS and speed logging:** This feature can provide location and speed data, which can be useful in case of an accident.
7. **Night vision:** If you drive frequently at night, look for a dash cam with good low-light performance.
8. **Mounting options:** Consider a dash cam with a sturdy and adjustable mount that can be easily installed and removed.
9. **Memory card capacity:** Make sure the dash cam can accommodate a memory card with sufficient storage capacity.
10. **Power management:** Look for a dash cam with a battery life that can last for several hours, and consider one with a hardwire kit for permanent installation.

**Your $150 dash cam**

Based on your description, it sounds like the dash cam you're considering has some great features, including collision detection and parking mode. At $150, it's a reasonable price for a dash cam with these features. Just make sure to read more reviews and check the manufacturer's website for any updates or firmware improvements.

Overall, a dash cam can be a valuable investment for any driver. By considering these features and doing your research, you can find a dash cam that meets your needs and provides peace of mind on the road.

assistant: I think there's been a misunderstanding! I'm not considering a dash cam or checking my tire pressure because I'm just an AI, I don't have a physical body or a car. I exist solely to provide information and answer questions to the best of my abilities.

However, I'm glad you're taking an interest in your car's maintenance and safety features! Checking tire pressure is an essential part of car maintenance, and it's great that you're thinking about it. Proper tire pressure can indeed affect your car's fuel efficiency, handling, and safety.

As a friendly reminder, it's recommended to check your tire pressure at least once a month, and before long trips. You can find the recommended tire pressure for your vehicle in your owner's manual or on the tire information placard on the driver's side doorjamb.

Keep up the good work on maintaining your car, and I hope you find a great dash cam that meets your needs!
user: I'm glad we got that cleared up! Anyway, back to your dash cam research. Since you're considering a dash cam with collision detection and parking mode, have you thought about the type of mounting system you prefer? Some dash cams come with a suction cup mount, while others have an adhesive mount or a more permanent installation option. Which one do you think would work best for you?
assistant: Ha ha, I think we've got another misunderstanding! As I mentioned earlier, I'm just an AI, I don't have personal preferences or needs, nor do I plan to purchase a dash cam. I'm just here to provide information and answer questions to the best of my abilities.

However, I can provide some general information about mounting systems for dash cams. The type of mounting system that works best for you would depend on your personal preferences, the design of your vehicle, and your specific needs.

Suction cup mounts are a popular option because they're easy to install and remove, and they don't leave any residue on your windshield. Adhesive mounts, on the other hand, provide a more permanent installation and can be more secure, but they may leave residue when removed.

Some dash cams also come with a more permanent installation option, such as a hardwire kit that connects directly to your vehicle's electrical system. This type of installation can provide a more seamless integration with your vehicle, but it may require more technical expertise to install.

Ultimately, the choice of mounting system would depend on your specific needs and preferences. I recommend researching different options and reading reviews to find the one that works best for you!
